lesson.menu_title: Talking About the World -- Opinions, News and Intelligent Conversation
lesson.menu_desc: Reading two sides of a real debate, then hedging your opinion with modals and concession -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Talking About the World
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 6 -- Talking About the World

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: an unfair preference for or against something
to give reasons for or against an idea
a particular way of seeing something
to accept that something is true or valid
to say something is true, often without proof yet
an unfair gap between rich and poor
to question whether an idea is right
a small but important difference in meaning

reading.rtitle: Two Sides of the Same Road
paras: Every year, more travelers reach places that were once off the map. It could be argued that this is a good thing. Tourism brings money, jobs, and attention to regions that need all three. In an emerging economy, a single popular valley can lift a whole town. Supporters point out that visitors also bring curiosity and respect, and that a village with guests has a reason to protect what makes it special.

There is another perspective, though. Critics claim that mass tourism can slowly damage the very thing people travel to see. Even though it creates income, it can raise prices for locals, strain fragile roads and services, and turn a living culture into a performance. It should be noted that the money does not always stay in the community; often it flows to large companies far away, deepening inequality rather than reducing it.

So who is right? Perhaps both sides are. That said, the honest answer has more nuance than either slogan admits. The places that do best are the ones with resilience: communities that welcome visitors on their own terms, that acknowledge the risks, and that challenge the idea that more tourists always means more progress. The road runs two ways, and how a town travels it may matter more than how many people arrive.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel-affairs feature, The World and the Road

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Mass tourism is always bad for remote places and should be stopped.
Both sides have a point; the honest answer has nuance, and communities with resilience do best.
Tourism is only about money, and culture does not matter.

items: The text says tourism only harms remote places.
The first paragraph argues the other side too: tourism brings money, jobs and a reason to protect what is special.
According to the text, tourism money always stays in the local community.
It says the money does not always stay; often it flows to large companies far away, deepening inequality.
The places that do best have resilience and welcome visitors on their own terms.
The last paragraph says exactly this: communities with resilience that welcome visitors on their own terms do best.
The writer gives a simple yes-or-no answer.
The writer says the honest answer has more nuance than either slogan admits.

parts: that it can raise prices for locals.
. The towns that do best have

gapfill.text: Notice the two tools of an intelligent opinion. HEDGES soften a claim so it does not sound absolute: 'It could be argued that...', 'There might be...', 'It should be noted that...'. CONCESSION markers admit the other side before you make your point: 'Even though...', 'That said...', 'On the other hand...'. Together they make you sound thoughtful, not aggressive.

bank.label: Useful language for an intelligent opinion
items: There might be a connection between ...

lf.title: Hedge (soften) or concession (admit the other side)?

lf.text: Notice: the green phrases HEDGE -- they make an opinion softer and less absolute (could be argued, might be). The blue phrases are CONCESSION -- they admit the other side before you make your point (even though, that said). A strong opinion, softened and balanced, sounds far more convincing than a loud one.

items: Give your opinion on mass tourism to remote places like Albania. Hedge it with 'It could be argued that ...' and add the other side with 'On the other hand, ...'
You disagree with someone at dinner. Challenge their idea politely: 'I see your point, but ...' and 'It should be noted that ...'
Talk about an emerging economy you know. Point out one benefit and acknowledge one risk. Use 'point out' and 'acknowledge'.
Give a balanced view on a current issue you follow (neuroscience, technology, geopolitics). Hedge with 'There might be ...' and add nuance.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = could be argued, 2 = Even though, 3 = point out, 4 = That said, 5 = nuance, 6 = resilience.
Model (hedged): 'It could be argued that tourism helps, but it should be noted that the money does not always stay local.'
Model (concession): 'Even though mass tourism creates jobs, it can raise prices. That said, communities with resilience can welcome visitors on their own terms.'
Hedge vs concession: hedges (could be argued, might be, you could say) soften a claim; concession markers (even though, that said, on the other hand) admit the other side before your point.
answerkey.note: A balanced opinion is not a weak one. Hedging and concession show you have considered the nuance -- they make a strong argument sound reasonable, not aggressive.

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it's Petra. I have been thinking about our conversation about tourism ever since. You made a fair point that I keep coming back to. I still believe it could be argued that visitors do more harm than good in fragile places, but you challenged me to see the other side, and I acknowledge that the money can matter. I read an article last night about a village in the Balkans that welcomes travelers on its own terms, and it stayed with me. Maybe the truth has more nuance than I wanted to admit. Anyway, thank you for the debate. It is rare to disagree with someone and still enjoy it.

Welcome back. Today, how to disagree well. Here is something worth remembering: the goal of a good argument is not to win, it is to understand. The best thinkers acknowledge the other side before they challenge it. They point out where their opponent is right, and only then explain where they differ. Notice how a small phrase, like that said, or on the other hand, can turn a fight into a conversation. And beware of your own bias. It could be argued that we all see the world through the lens of where we come from. The most interesting people are not the ones with the strongest opinions. They are the ones who can hold two ideas at once, and still make up their mind.
